coredata.dc:title: Halloysite nanotubes-carbon dots hybrids multifunctional nanocarrier with positive cell target ability as a potential non-viral vector for oral gene therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Massaro, Marina
Barone, Giampaolo
Biddeci, Giuseppa
Cavallaro, Giuseppe
Di Blasi, Francesco
Lazzara, Giuseppe
Nicotra, Giuseppe
Spinella, Corrado
Spinelli, Gaetano
Riela, Serena
coredata.dc:description:
Hypothesis The use of non-viral vectors for gene therapy is hindered by their lower transfection efficiency and their lacking of self-track ability.
Experiments This study aims to investigate the biological properties of halloysite nanotubes-carbon dots hybrid and its potential use as non-viral vector for oral gene therapy. The morphology and the chemical composition of the halloysite hybrid were investigated by means of high angle annular dark field scanning TEM and electron energy loss spectroscopy techniques, respectively. The cytotoxicity and the antioxidant activity were investigated by standard methods (MTS, DPPH and H2O2, respectively) using human cervical cancer HeLa cells as model. Studies of cellular uptake were carried out by fluorescence microscopy. Finally, we investigated the loading and release ability of the hybrid versus calf thymus DNA by fluorescence microscopy, circular dichroism, dynamic light scattering and ζ-potential measurements.
Findings All investigations performed confirmed the existence of strong electrostatic interactions between the DNA and the halloysite hybrid, so it shows promise as a multi-functional cationic non-viral vector that has also possesses intracellular tracking capability and promising in vitro antioxidant potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Halloysite nanotubes
Carbon dots
DNA interaction
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the HNT-CDs hybrid
Interaction studies of HNT-CDs and ct-DNA
Loading, surface charge and aqueous dynamic behavior of HNT-CDs/ct-DNA complex

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cartoon representation of HNT-CDs multifunctional nanocarrier.

(a,b) Carbon 2D chemical map (green) and Z-contrast image (red scale). (c,d) EELS spectrum showing the presence of CK, NK and OK ionization edges respectively taken in the area containing HNT-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
MTS test for cell viability of HeLa cells cultured for 24 h in presence of HNT-CDs nanomaterial.

Fluorescence microscopy images of (a) HeLa cells; (b) HeLa cells treated with HNT-CDs (10 μg mL−1) and (c,d) HeLa cells treated with CDs (50 μg mL−1) at 20× (c) and 40× (d) of magnification, respectively.
Flow cytometry analysis in HeLa cells treated with HNT-CDs.

(a) Inhibition percentage of the free radical DPPH by a dispersion of HNT-CDs in H2O/DMSO (95:5 v/v). Relative errors are in a range of ±0.2 (results correspond to the mean ± standard error mean of two independent assays with two replicas each); (b) Antioxidant activity of HNT-CDs in HeLa cells.

(a) Trend of the HNT-CDs (0.8 mg mL−1) fluorescence intensity at 460 nm as a function of ct-DNA concentration (0–8.5 × 10−5 M); (b) trend of the fluorescence intensity of EB (1.1 × 10−5 M) complexed with ct-DNA (1.1 × 10−5 M) as a function of [HNT-CDs] (0–6 mg mL−1).

Trend of the circular dichroism of (a) ct-DNA (1.7 × 10−5 M) in the presence of HNT-CDs hybrid (from 0 to 1.8 mg mL−1) and (b) ct-DNA/EB complex (1:1 M ratio, 1.7 × 10−5 M) in the presence of HNT-CDs hybrid (from 0 to 2 mg mL−1).
Kinetic release of ct-DNA from HNT-CDs/ct-DNA complex in (a) HCl 0.1 N and (b) TRIS-Cl buffer pH 7.4.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of the HNT-CDs nanomaterial.
ζ-potential and hydrodynamic diameter of HNT-CDs and DNA/ HNT-CDs nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Halloysite nanotubes-carbon dots hybrids multifunctional nanocarrier with positive cell target ability as a potential non-viral vector for oral gene therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dipartimento di Fisica e Chimica (DiFC), University of Palermo, Viale delle Scienze, Ed. 17, 90128 Palermo, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CNR-IMM, Zona Industriale Strada VIII, 5, 95121 Catania, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CNR-IMM, Zona Industriale Strada VIII, 5, 95121 Catania, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of non-viral vectors for gene therapy is hindered by their lower transfection efficiency and their lacking of self-track ability.

This study aims to investigate the biological properties of halloysite nanotubes-carbon dots hybrid and its potential use as non-viral vector for oral gene therapy. The morphology and the chemical composition of the halloysite hybrid were investigated by means of high angle annular dark field scanning TEM and electron energy loss spectroscopy techniques, respectively. The cytotoxicity and the antioxidant activity were investigated by standard methods (MTS, DPPH and H2O2, respectively) using human cervical cancer HeLa cells as model. Studies of cellular uptake were carried out by fluorescence microscopy. Finally, we investigated the loading and release ability of the hybrid versus calf thymus DNA by fluorescence microscopy, circular dichroism, dynamic light scattering and ζ-potential measurements.

All investigations performed confirmed the existence of strong electrostatic interactions between the DNA and the halloysite hybrid, so it shows promise as a multi-functional cationic non-viral vector that has also possesses intracellular tracking capability and promising in vitro antioxidant potential.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few years, the introduction of genetic material into cells (gene therapy) has attracted considerable attention as a treatment for a number of genetic and chronic diseases including certain viral infections, cystic fibrosis and some types of cancer [1]. Among the different therapies, oral gene therapy offers remarkable advantages with respect to traditional procedures, such as injections, as it ensures high patient compliance and ease of administration. Usually, viral vectors are used as safe gene delivery systems in the clinical trials, while only 4.4% of the gene carriers employs non-viral vectors, which are safer. Unfortunately, the non-viral vectors clinical application is limited due to their lower transfection efficiency and their lack of self-tracking ability. To overcome these limitations some efforts are being made to improve the synthesis of novel cationic ligands such as liposomes, lipids, peptides and so on [2,3]. More recently, carbon dots have received significant attention since they possess low toxicity, good water solubility, chemical stability and a variety of protonable chemical groups on their surface. Therefore they are promising as nanocarrier for gene delivery applications [4].

Clay minerals are widely used in the biomedical field [5] and are known to protect genetic material during its delivery through the body, especially in the gastro-intestinal tract, where DNA damaged [6]. Among these, halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) have attracted considerable attention for their ability to entrap, to protect and to provide sustained release of drugs [7–10]. Chemically, they are an aluminosilicate clay with a predominantly tubular structure and a chemical formula of Al2Si2O5(OH)4·nH2O. Generally, HNTs have a length in the range of 0.2–1.5 μm, with an inner and outer diameters in the ranges of 10–30 nm and 40–70 nm, respectively. Halloysite has positively charged lumen, which consists mostly of aluminium hydroxide, whereas the external surface, which consists of silicon dioxide, is negatively charged. Thanks to this different chemical composition several modifications of the inner and/or outer surface can be performed by exploiting both supramolecular interactions and the covalent linkage of suitable chemical groups [11,12]. Furthermore, HNTs are biocompatible and safe nanomaterials for a large number of living organisms, able to penetrate into the cell membrane arranging themselves around the cell nuclei, as demonstrated by several in vitro and in vivo studies [13–17].

Recently, we have synthesized fluorescent HNTs by the direct covalent linkage of nitrogen doped carbon dots (HNT-CDs) on the halloysite external surface [18]. These materials have shown excellent photoluminescent properties which could permit their use in many applications especially in the biological field.
Herein, we investigate the potential use of HNT-CDs nanomaterials as a novel non-viral vector for an oral gene delivery system (Fig. 1 ).
In particular we evaluated the biological properties of the HNT-CDs in terms of cytotoxicity, antioxidant properties and cellular uptake.

Since it is known that CDs could be uptaken into cell nuclei, we have also studied the HNT-CDs interaction with calf thymus DNA (ct-DNA), chosen as a double-helical DNA model, by fluorescence spectroscopy, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and circular dichroism.
Finally, envisaging the possibility to use HNT-CDs hybrid as an oral non-viral vector, we investigated the loading and the release of ct-DNA from the HNT-CDs carrier.
All reagents were purchased from Merck and used without further purification.

Lyophilized calf thymus DNA (Fluka, BioChemika) was resuspended in 1.0 mM tris-hydroxymethyl-aminomethane (Tris-HCl) pH = 7.5 and dialyzed as described in the literature. DNA concentration, expressed in monomers units ([DNAphosphate]), was determined by UV spectrophotometry using 7000 M−1 cm−1 as molar absorption coefficient at 260 nm.

Thermogravimetry analyses were carried out by means of a Q5000 IR apparatus (TA Instruments). The measurements were conducted under nitrogen atmosphere (the gas flows were set at 25 and 10 cm3 min−1 for the sample and the balance, respectively) from 25 to 700 °C. The heating rate was set at 20 °C min−1.
IR spectra (KBr) were recorded with an Agilent Technologies Cary 630 FT-IR spectrometer. Specimens for these measurements were prepared by mixing 5 mg of the sample powder with 100 mg of KBr.
ζ-Potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed by using a a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments) at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C.

Steady-state fluorescence spectra were acquired using a JASCO FP-777W spectrofluorometer. Excitation and emission slits were 1.5 and 3 nm, respectively, with an emission interval ranging between 300 and 700 nm.
UV–vis measurements were performed using a Beckmann DU 650 spectrometer.
Circular dichroism spectra were recorded with a Jasco J715 spectropolarimeter, under nitrogen flow.

HNT-CDs hybrid was synthetized as previously reported [18]. Briefly, the tartaric acid functionalized HNT (65 mg), EDA (100 mL) and water (300 mL) were heated at 240 °C, at 200 W for 3 min in the presence of a catalytic amount of TEA. After this, the solid powder was collected, rinsed several times with ethanol, to remove the unreacted reagents and dried at 80 °C under vacuum.

The antioxidant activity of the HNT-CDs hybrid was evaluated using the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH•) method. The interaction of HNT-CDs with DPPH radicals resulted in fast decoloration of the DPPH solution. Radical scavenging activity was determined following as follow. The HNT-CDs nanomaterial (concentration ranging from 0 to 6.25 mg mL−1) was dispersed in 1 mL of a mixture H2O/DMSO (95:5). 1 mL of DPPH solution (1.5 × 10−4 M) in a mixture H2O/DMSO (95:5) was then added to the tested sample. The mixture was then vigorously mixed and allowed to stand at room temperature in the dark for 60 min. The inhibition of free radical DPPH in percent (I%) was calculated as:
(1)
I % =
A c
-
A
HNT - C D s
A c
× 100
where Ac is the absorbance of the DDPH solution without HNT-CDs (λ = 523 nm), and AHNT-CDs is the absorbance of the test sample. All tests were carried out in duplicate, and inhibition percentages were reported as mean ± SD.

HeLa cells (Human cervical cancer HeLa cells) were purchased by ATCC (American Type Culture Collection) and cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium supplemented with 5% fetal bovine serum and 2 mM l-glutamine, 100 units per mL penicillin and 100 mg/mL streptomycin (Gibco Life Technologies) and incubated in a humidified atmosphere at 37 °C in 5% CO2.

In order to study cytotoxicity and antioxidant activity of HNT-CDs hybrid, HeLa cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well on 96-well plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Afterwards, cells were treated with H2O2 (100 μM) to induce oxidative stress, and with different concentrations of HNT-CDs ranging from 0.04 mg/mL to 2 mg/mL and incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. After treatment, cell viability was assayed using 3-(4,5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3 carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium (MTS), added to the cells and allowed to develop for 1 h at 37 °C. Colorimetric measurements were performed at 490 nm using an ELISA plate reader.

All experiments were performed in triplicate and repeated at least twice. The results were expressed as mean ± SEM. The responses were calculated as a percentage compared to the untreated group results.

HeLa cells were seeded at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well on chamber slide for cell culturing and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Subsequently, cells were treated for 4 h with HNT-CDs at different concentrations ranging from 5 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL. Once finished the treatment, cells were washed with PBS (1×) and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min. After this passage cells were washed with PBS (1×) and nuclei were stained with 2-(4-Amidinophenyl)-6-indolecarbamidine dihydrochloride, 4′,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI dihydrochloride) for 20 min. After nuclear staining, cells were further washed with PBS (1×) and the slide was observed under fluorescence microscope.
(2)
Flow cytometry

HeLa cells with density of 2.5 × 105 cells per well were cultured in 6-well plates for 24 h and subsequently treated with different concentration of HNT-CDs, ranging from 10 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL. After different periods of incubation (namely 30 min, 2 h, 4 h and 24 h), the cells were washed with PBS and collected in tubes for flow cytometry. Afterward, cells were suspended in FACS buffer and analyzed with flow cytometry (FL1 and FL2 channels).

The concentration of ct-DNA was fixed at 1.7 × 10−5 M while different concentrations of the HNT-CDs hybrid were in the range 0–2 mg/mL. All circular dichroism spectra were recorded at room temperature in Tris-HCl buffer (2.0 × 10−3 M, pH 7.4).

In order to understand the interaction pattern of the HNT-CDs hybrid with ct-DNA the fluorescence titration method was used. Fluorescence measurements were carried out under the Tris–HCl buffer (2.0 × 10−3 M, pH 7.4) at a wavelength range of 376–576 nm (λex = 325 nm) by keeping the concentration of hybrid constant (2.0 ± 0.1 mg mL−1) and changing the concentration of ct-DNA from 0 to 8.72 × 10−5 M.

The competitive fluorescence study of reaction between pHNT or HNT-CDs hybrid and ethidium bromide (EB) with ct-DNA (1.1 × 10−5 M, molar ratio 1:1) were performed in Tris–HCl buffer solution (2.0 × 10−3 M, pH 7.4) at room temperature. This part of the experiment was carried out by titration of constant amounts of the DNA–EB systems with addition of different amounts of p-HNT or HNT-CDs hybrid and analysis of the changes in fluorescence intensities (λem = 604.5 nm) after 24 h. The fluorescence spectra of the DNA-EB were recorded in the wavelength ranges of 550–700 nm (λex = 490 nm).

The fluorescence quenching data of EB in the presence of different HNT-CDs concentration were analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation as follows:
(2)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
SV
·
[ Q ]
where I 0 and I represent the steady-state fluorescence intensities of DNA/EB complex in the absence and in the presence of HNT-CDs nanomaterial; while KSV and Q represent the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and the concentration of the quencher, respectively.

The release of ct-DNA from HNT-CDs/ct-DNA complex was performed as follows: 25 mg of the sample were transferred into a dialysis membrane (Medicell International Ltd MWCO 12–14,000 with diameter of 21.5 mm) and wet with 1 mL of the release medium. The membrane was then put in a round bottom flask containing 9 mL of the release medium at 37 °C and stirred. At fixed times, 1 mL of the release medium was withdrawn and analyzed. To keep constant the volume of the release medium, 1 mL of fresh solution was added each time to replace the withdrawn one. The ct-DNA concentration in the solution, expressed in monomers units ([DNAphosphate]) was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometry using the Lambert-Beer law.

Total amount of ct-DNA released (Ft) was calculated as follows:
(3)
F t
=
V m
C t
+
∑
i = 0
t - 1
V a
C i
where Vm and Ct are volume and moles of the molecule at time t. Va is the volume of the sample withdrawn and Ci is DNA moles at time i (i < t).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the HNT-CDs hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the HNT-CDs hybrid was synthesized by following a procedure reported previously by us [18]. Briefly, luminescent HNT-CDs nanomaterials were obtained from tartaric acid functionalized halloysite (1) in the presence of EDA as passivant agent (Scheme 1 ) [18]. Following this procedure, the percent loading of the organic matter onto the HNT surface, estimated by thermogravimetric analysis, was 3.50 wt%. The physico-chemical characterization showed that the nanomaterial possesses several oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the particle surface with some primary amino groups, which could be beneficial for biological applications [18].

It is known that, for biomedical applications, the size of the therapeutic nanomaterial is crucial since it could affect the cell internalization. Small nanoparticles indeed can be eliminated by renal filtration during circulation in the human body [19]. Thus, the HNT-CDs hybrid size was investigated by DLS measurements that revealed an apparent hydrodynamic radius of the nanomaterial of ca. 300 nm.

To gain additional structural information about the HNT-CDs hybrids, the physical and chemical characterization of the material has been performed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by High Angle Annular Dark Field Scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) and Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS) techniques, respectively (Fig. 2 ). The former gives Z-contrast images allowing discrimination between the light and heavy atoms compounds, the latter allows both chemical identifications and bonding arrangement of the atoms. By the combination of the two techniques a 2D-chemical map of the different elements may be extracted. Here we report the carbon maps of two different structures over imposed on the corresponding STEM Z-contrast image (Fig. S.1). They show two kinds of systems, one with the presence a cluster lying upon the HNT-CDs surface (Fig. 2a), which could be due to supramolecular interactions between the different dots on the HNT surface, and another where a carbon thin layer is covering the entire HNT-CDs, respectively (Fig. 2b).

The detailed analysis of the EELS spectra at carbon K edge show a sharp peak at around 285 eV corresponding to 1 s-π* transition, related to π bonding, who is taller than the one expected for the a-C, thus entailing a high number of sp2 bonds (Fig. 2c-d). Besides this peak, a broad 1 s-σ* and blunted peak is also present at around 290 eV, this shape is corresponding to disordered CC bonds. Nitrogen and oxygen K edges signature on the spectrum are also visible.
Diffraction pattern and his intensity profile showing the two main rings, the specimens look amorphous (Fig. S.2).

Since an ideal carrier requires, besides suitable chemical and delivery properties, low cytotoxicity, the in vitro effects of HNT-CDs hybrid on cell lines, were investigated by the standard MTS assay using HeLa cells as model. The cell viability was examined upon exposure to different concentrations of HNT-CDs (ranging from 0 to 30 mg mL−1), as depicted in Fig. 3 . It reveals that the nanomaterial exhibited extremely low cytotoxicity, (i.e., viability > 95%) after 24 h incubation, even at high concentration of 30 mg mL−1 of HNT-CDs according to data reported for pristine HNT [16].

These findings are due to the utilization of both the natural HNTs and tartaric acid as precursor, combined to a green microwave synthesis without the addition of any toxic solvents or harsh treatment processes, giving rise to a nanomaterial with high biocompatibility [20].

As previously reported by us [18], the HNT-CDs nanomaterials have shown the typical excitation-wavelength emission, as a result of the different functional groups present on the HNT-CDs surface. In an aqueous dispersion, a broad emission peak at 420 nm is observed when the sample is excited at an optimal excitation wavelength (325 nm), with a fluorescence quantum yield of 17% using 1,9 diphenyl anthracene as standard. The emission of HNT-CDs synthesized is strictly dependent on their excitation wavelength. In particular by changing the excitation wavelength from 245 to 385 nm, it is observed a shift of the fluorescence emission band from 425 to 460 nm with a concomitant decrease of the fluorescence intensity. On the basis of these findings, the HNT-CDs nanomaterial can be used as efficient imaging agent in biological applications.

As a proof of concept, the aqueous dispersion of HNT-CDs was used for cancer cell imaging, using HeLa cells as model. We studied the intracellular distribution of HNT-CDs in living cells by fluorescence microscopy. The fluorescence signal of the nanomaterial can be clearly observed inside the cells (Fig. 4 b). Close observations revealed that the HNT-CDs were distributed mainly in the perinuclear region and in the cytoplasmic area, as expected for pristine halloysite. It is noteworthy that cell nuclei showed also a weak fluorescence. In contrast, no fluorescence signals were detected in the control cells without HNT-CDs labelling, as shown in Fig. 4a. In conclusion, fluorescence signal indicates that HNT-CDs can be internalized in the cells. To better understand the different behaviour of HNT-CDs nanomaterial in comparison with pristine HNT, HeLa cells were also incubated with CDs, prepared as reference materials. As it is possible to observe from Fig. 4c-d, also in this case the nanomaterial is mainly localized in the cytoplasm and some of them were localized in the cell nuclei, too. Therefore, the functionalization of halloysite nanotubes with carbon-nanodots allows the synthesis of a biocompatible material with improved cellular uptake. The new material that has very good capacity for internalization and easily aggregates in the perinuclear region, could represent a new ally to be used for the administration of drugs in pharmaceutical and biomedical areas thanks to the possibility of localizing it through its fluorescent properties.

Furthermore, investigations on the fluorescence intensity distributions by flow cytometry (with FL1-H and FL2-H channels) demonstrate that HNT-CDs can readily enter into HeLa cells. Surprisingly, the HNT-CDs enters almost totally after only 30 min as showed by the shift in the fluorescence intensity from the second decade, both in the FL1-H (positive events 77%) and in the FL2-H (positive events 78%) (Fig. 5 ). These results are in contrast to that reported in literature regarding the HNT nanomaterial cellular uptake. In the last case indeed, it is observed an HNT uptake only after 24 h of treatment [21]. The obtained findings could be due to the presence of primary amino groups on the HNT-CDs surface, which could promote the nanomaterial uptake by improving the interaction with cell membrane.

By increasing the incubation time, only a slight increase, in the fluorescence intensity, was observed whether FL1-H (positive events 79%) and FL2-H (positive events 83%). Finally, after 24 h of incubation time, it is possible to observe the fluorescence intensity is in the range of 86% for FL1-H and 88% for FL2-H, confirming that the most of the HNT-CDs hybrid is able to penetrate the cellular membrane after 30 min of treatment.

The radical trapping ability of the HNT-CDs nanomaterial was assessed by studying the reaction with 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH). This radical is commonly used to evaluate the antioxidant properties of natural products, as the purple free radical is transformed by reductants to the yellow hydrazine through a formal hydrogen atom transfer reaction.

The antioxidant capacity of the HNT-CDs hybrid in a mixture H2O/DMSO (95:5 v/v) was evaluated by plotting the percentage of inhibition of the free radical scavenging capacity of DPPH against the concentrations of different dispersions of HNT-CDs (from 0.5 to 6.25 mg mL−1) (Fig. 6 a). The hybrid synthesized showed good antioxidant activity that increases with the increasing of HNT-CDs concentration (IC50 = 2.95 ± 0.2 mg mL−1) while the pristine HNT did not exhibit any radical scavenging activity [22].

Oxidative stress is defined as an alteration in the balance between the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and antioxidant defense of biological systems able to remove them [23]. ROS includes hydroxyl radical (
OH), superoxide (O2−) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and are steadily produced in aerobic organisms. Oxidative stress leads to many pathophysiological conditions, ROS can react with nucleic acids, proteins and lipids causing oxidative stress and damage in these macromolecules leading to pathogenesis of age-related and chronic disease.

N-acetyl-cysteine (NAC) is an antioxidant that is able to minimize oxidative stress and the downstream negative effects thought to be associated with oxidative stress. The antioxidant effect is due to the ability of NAC to act as a reduced glutathione (GSH) precursor; GSH is a well-known direct antioxidant and a substrate of several antioxidant enzymes [24].

The antioxidant activity of the hybrid was evaluated in human cervix adenocarcinoma HeLa cell line. The cells were treated for 24 h with H2O2 to induce oxidative stress and N-acetylcysteine (NAC) as control of antioxidant activity. Furthermore, HeLa cells were treated also with several concentrations of HNT-CDs ranging from 0.04 to 1 mg mL−1 for 24 h. The cell viability and the antioxidant activity were evaluated by MTS assay (Fig. 6b).

As it is possible to observe, the oxidative stress induced by treatment with H2O2 was restored by increasing concentration of antioxidant, with a cell viability in the range of 40–100%. These results also confirm that in vitro the HNT-CDs hybrid possesses antioxidant properties which can deal with the ROS production induced by H2O2.

As previously reported, the surface of HNT-CDs contains several oxygen and nitrogen functional groups (such as hydroxyl, ketones, amines and aldehydes). These groups could act as proton donors and transfer free electrons to the carbon dots bulk structure. Therefore, the HNT-CDs could be degraded by the release of acidic residues like carbonates and bicarbonates and could scavenge free radicals acting as antioxidant. Similar findings were reported in literature for carbon nanodots from date molasses [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction studies of HNT-CDs and ct-DNA
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the possibility of use the HNT-CDs hybrid as oral non-viral vector we performed some studies to evaluate the interaction of ct-DNA with the developed nanomaterials by means of fluorescence spectroscopy, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and circular dichroism.

Firstly, 5.0 mL of a solution containing a fixed concentration of HNT-CDs (2.0 ± 0.1 mg/mL) was titrated with various concentrations of ct-DNA (from 0 to 8.72 × 10−5 M). The effect of ct-DNA on HNT-CDs hybrid fluorescence intensity at room temperature is shown in Fig. 7 a. As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity of the HNT-CDs was quenched by the addition of increasing concentration of ct-DNA, indicating the existence of an interaction between ct-DNA and HNT-CDs hybrid.

The binding constant of the complex HNT-CDs/ct-DNA was determined by plotting the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm as a function of the molar concentration of ct-DNA [26] (Fig. S.3). The obtained Kb value was (1.22 ± 0.09) × 105 M−1, indicating that HNT-CDs hybrid efficiently binds to the DNA double-helix.

It is known that ethidium bromide (EB) is a molecule which can intercalate in the DNA base pairs and therefore it can be used as probe to obtain information about the interaction of an unknown system with DNA. When EB is bound to DNA, its fluorescence intensity increases due to the absorption of energy from excited nucleotides. Therefore, competitive binding displacement of the EB bound to ct-DNA by means of HNT-CDs hybrid was carried out to further clarify the DNA–binding mode of the HNT-CDs hybrid at the excitation wavelength of 490 nm.

The effect of HNT-CDs hybrid on ct-DNA/EB fluorescence intensity at room temperature is shown in Fig. 7b. The spectra were recorded after 24 h of the preparation of dispersions. No variation in fluorescence intensity was observed in dispersions freshly prepared. With increasing the concentration of HNT-CDs hybrid (from 0 to 6 mg mL−1) the fluorescence intensity of ct-DNA/EB decreases, presumably as a consequence of a rearrangement of the double helix of ct-DNA around the halloysite nanotubes, which causes partial extrusion of the EB molecules from the DNA intercalation pockets. Indeed, it has been reported that the interaction between DNA and HNTs induces a reorientation of the phosphate groups in DNA and an association of them with the outer surface of the nanotubes [27,28]. The fluorescence quenching data of EB in the presence of different HNT-CDs concentrations were analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation obtaining a KSV constant value of 3.45 ± 0.21 mL mg−1.
Similar results were obtained by adding, to the ct-DNA/EB complex different concentrations of pristine HNTs (from 0 to 6 mg mL−1).

In the light of the above results it can be concluded that the formation of halloysite/ct-DNA complex is preferentially due to the interactions with the tubes independently of their functionalization. Whilst, the carbon-dots on the HNTs surface are responsible of the nuclear uptake that is not observed in the pristine one (Fig. 4b).

DSC studies confirmed the significant interactions between HNT-CDs and ct-DNA. The ct-DNA solution showed an exothermic peak centered at ca. 60 °C that can be attributed to the unfolding of a nucleic acid helical structure [29]. This transition was shifted to a larger temperature in the presence of the HNT-CDs nanomaterial. Specifically, the HNT-CDs/ct-DNA suspension exhibited an exothermic peak centred at ca. 85 °C. This effect could be related to the ct-DNA adsorption onto the HNT-CDs nanomaterial.

To confirm both the DNA-binding mode of the halloysite in the HNT-CDs/ct-DNA complex and that the DNA structure does not undergo unfolding after interaction with the hybrid, circular dichroism experiments were carried out. Generally, the circular dichroism of ct-DNA shows a positive band at 275 nm, corresponding to the base stacking, and a negative band at 245 nm, due to helicity, which together represent characteristics of ct-DNA in right-handed B-form [30]. Classical intercalators tend to enhance the intensities of bands due to strong base stacking interactions and stable ct-DNA conformations, while groove binding and electrostatic interactions with molecules cause less perturbation of the base stacking and helicity bands [31].

The circular dichroism spectra of ct-DNA with the addition of different concentrations of the HNT-CDs hybrid are shown in Fig. 8 a. In the presence of the HNT-CDs hybrid, the intensity of negative peaks (245 nm) in the spectrum of ct-DNA was marginally decreased; whilst no relevant variations occurred at 275 nm. Accordingly, it is possible to confirm that after interaction with the hybrid no drastic conformational changes or destabilization occur to the B-DNA structure.

To confirm that the driving force that lead to the formation the halloysite/ct-DNA complex is essentially due to electrostatic interactions, the circular dichroism of ct-DNA/EB complex in the presence of different concentrations of the HNT-CDs hybrid were also recorded (Fig. 8b). In the spectrum of the EB/DNA complex, besides the typical bands of the ct-DNA, it is possible to observe a new band, from 300 to 350 nm due to the induced circular dichroism of EB bound to ct-DNA. After addition of a little amount of HNT-CDs hybrid in the complex, a slight monotonous decrease in the latter band was observed, confirming that no halloysite-DNA intercalation occurs, but that nevertheless the halloysite-DNA interaction induces a rearrangement of the double helix and a partial release of the intercalated EB, in good agreement with the fluorescence results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading, surface charge and aqueous dynamic behavior of HNT-CDs/ct-DNA complex
ce:section.ce:para: The loading of ct-DNA onto the HNT-CDs hybrid was carried out by mixing the hybrid with a highly concentrated ct-DNA solution. Then, the obtained suspension was left to stir at room temperature [32]. After loading, the HNT-CDs/ct-DNA complex was washed with water in order to remove free ct-DNA.

The amount of ct-DNA adsorbed onto the HNT-CDs nanomaterial was calculated by thermogravimetric (TG) analysis. To this purpose, TG curves for HNT-CDs/ct-DNA and their pure components (ct-DNA and HNT-CDs) were collected and quantitatively analyzed. According to the TG data, the ct-DNA loading is 6.0 wt%.

The adsorption of ct-DNA did not significantly alter the surface charge and aqueous dynamic behaviour of HNT-CDs nanomaterial as demonstrated by the ζ-potential and aqueous hydrodynamic diameter values (Table 1 ).

Regarding the ζ-potential, the small change induced by the ct-DNA adsorption could indicate that the interactions between ct-DNA and HNT-CDs are not mainly driven by electrostatic forces. On the other hand, the increase in the absolute ζ-potential value agrees with the adsorption of the ct-DNA polyanion which partially neutralises the positive surface of HNT. The hydrodynamic diameters of both nanomaterials are similar to that of pure HNTs [33] avoiding the formation of aggregates between the modified nanotubes.

The HNT-CDs/ct-DNA solid complex was further characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S.4). It was found that the HNT-CDs/ct-DNA complex shows all the characteristic peaks of HNT-CDs nanomaterial, in particular the peaks at ca. 1600 cm−1, indicative of the presence of CO bonds, a broad peak at 3100 cm−1, attributable to the vibration stretching band of OH/NH bonding and the CN (1492 and 1437 cm−1) an d CN/CC (1557 cm−1) vibration stretching bands [18].

Besides these, the complex shows in its spectrum the presence of additional peaks, at ca. 2960 cm−1 relative to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of two endocyclic CH2 moieties at C-5 of the 2-deoxy-d-ribose of ct-DNA [34].

Envisaging some possibility to transfer ct-DNA into cells, it is important to verify that adsorbed ct-DNA can actually be the released from the HNT-CDs hybrid. Therefore, the kinetic release of ct-DNA from HNT-CDs was performed by dialysis bag diffusion method in different release media, mimicking physiological conditions, namely pH 1.0 and TRIS-HCl buffer at pH 7.4, respectively, at 37 °C. As it is possible to observe from Fig. 9 , the ct-DNA shows sustained release from HNT-CDs hybrid over the time. It is interesting to note that the amount ct-DNA released in TRIS-HCl buffer is lower than that released in acidic medium; where the release was sustained for the first 100 min and becomes relevant after ca. 3 h. Since the driving force in the formation of a complex between DNA and HNT-CDs hybrid are the electrostatic interactions among the positive charged HNT-CDs hybrid surface and the negative ct-DNA, the different behavior at the two different pH could be explained by the existence of electrostatic repulsions between the DNA and HNT-CDs surface (both protonate) [35] in acidic medium, which could accelerate the release rate.

On the basis of the obtained results, the HNT-CDs can be used as a good alternative to delivery DNA in the body by oral dosage for gene therapy purposes, since it ensures a low amount of DNA released in physiological media, avoiding, for example, the DNA degradation during the gastro-intestinal transit.

In summary, in this study we have prepared HNT-CDs hybrid with low cytotoxicity by a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of tartaric acid and ethylene diamine. The obtained novel hybrid nanomaterials possess the peculiarity of both halloysite and carbon nanodots, showing the remarkable advantage to be made from a natural material, available in large amount at low cost such as halloysite, which possesses low cytotoxicity and the ability to penetrate the cellular membrane that it is essential for a targeted delivery. All of these features hold a great promise for the oral non-viral gene delivery.

The chemical composition and the morphology of the hybrid were highlighted by HAADF-STEM and EELS techniques and its potential use as non-viral vector for gene therapy was investigated. In this context, we studied the interaction of the hybrid with ct-DNA, chosen as model. All investigations performed confirm the existence of strong electrostatic interactions between the ct-DNA and the halloysite hybrid. Circular dichroism measurements showed that the ct-DNA interaction with halloysite did not cause alteration in the DNA structure and by means of DSC studies it was found that the DNA molecule benefits, in terms of thermal stability, from the presence of HNT-CDs. The efficacy of HNT-CDs as non-viral vector for DNA was also proved by in vitro release experiments performed at pH 1.0 and 7.4 and 37 °C in order to simulate the physiological conditions. We observed that ct-DNA is slowly released through a dialysis membrane in physiological media. This aspect could be crucial for an oral treatment, since it ensures the delivery of DNA through the body.

Furthermore, the obtained HNT-CDs hybrid could be considered a multi-functional cationic non-viral vector since, besides the potential gene delivery capacity, it has shown by tunable fluorescence studies intracellular tracking of the delivered molecules and promising in vitro antioxidant performances.

Therefore, the hybrid system so-obtained could be potentially serviceable as non-viral vector in the field of theranostics, bioimaging, and smart materials, and we forecast an optimistic start to other futuristic applications.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.05.062.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
